does: This paper introduces a stakeholder-centric benchmark for evaluating prompt-injection attacks against LLM-based web agents operating over untrusted online content. Rather than simply measuring attack success rates, the work systematically categorizes and attributes harms to different affected parties—users, sellers, platforms—demonstrating that a single injection attack can produce asymmetric consequences across stakeholders. The benchmark decomposes attacks into concrete objectives and maps how different agent architectures, configurations, and victim characteristics modulate attack effectiveness and impact distribution. Through empirical evaluation on real-world web scenarios, the work reveals that existing security metrics obscure the true risk landscape by ignoring whose interests are actually harmed by successful exploits.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against language models and autonomous agents by shifting perspective from purely technical feasibility toward sociotechnical impact assessment. It builds on earlier prompt-injection studies that identified manipulation vulnerabilities in LLM systems, but responds to their limitation of treating harms monolithically. The paper connects to broader literatures on AI safety evaluation, security benchmarking, and stakeholder analysis in sociotechnical systems. It also relates to work examining how design choices in agent systems affect robustness and safety outcomes, and draws on frameworks from security engineering that distinguish between threat actors, victims, and affected third parties.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more nuanced security evaluation of deployed AI systems by prioritizing stakeholder vulnerability over attack novelty. It enables future work to design defenses and deployment policies tailored to specific threat models rather than generic robustness. The stakeholder perspective could inform responsible deployment practices, insurance models, and regulatory frameworks for autonomous agents in commerce and customer service. The work also points toward richer threat modeling for multiparty systems where single actions ripple asymmetrically across users, businesses, and platforms, potentially influencing how organizations prioritize and allocate resources for AI safety interventions and mitigation strategies.
